title: TaskFlow server gets slower throughout the day, requires daily restart
body:
Our self-hosted TaskFlow instance starts the day running fine but gets progressively slower. By late afternoon, page loads take 10+ seconds and the API times out. We've been restarting the server every evening for the past week. Memory usage on the server climbs from ~500MB at startup to 4GB+ by end of day. We have about 200 active users.

I've attached a screenshot of our Grafana dashboard showing the memory climb. The pattern is very consistent day-to-day.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for the detailed report and the Grafana data — the consistent memory climb pattern is really helpful.

To help narrow this down, could you share some environment details?

- What **version of TaskFlow** are you running?
- What **OS and runtime** is the server on (e.g., Ubuntu 22.04, Node 18, Docker container)?
- What **database** are you using (PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite), and is it on the same host or separate?
- Any **reverse proxy** in front of TaskFlow (Nginx, Apache, etc.)?

This will help us determine whether this is a known issue in a specific version or configuration.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: We're running TaskFlow v2.3 — we upgraded about a week ago, which is actually right around when this problem started, now that I think about it. The server is on Ubuntu 22.04, it's an 8GB RAM VM. I'm not sure what runtime you mean exactly but it's the standard install from the docs. We're using PostgreSQL on a separate host. And yes, we have Nginx in front of it as a reverse proxy, but that's been unchanged for months.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body: [RESOLVED] {"title":"Memory leak causes progressive server slowdown after upgrading to TaskFlow v2.3","problem":"Self-hosted TaskFlow instance experiences a consistent daily memory leak, with RAM usage climbing from ~500MB at startup to 4GB+ by end of day. This causes page loads to exceed 10 seconds and API timeouts, requiring a daily server restart. The problem affects approximately 200 active users.","root_cause_hypothesis":"A memory leak was introduced in TaskFlow v2.3. The reporter noted the problem started approximately one week ago, coinciding with their upgrade from a prior version to v2.3. The consistent daily pattern and linear memory growth strongly suggest a per-request or per-session resource that is not being properly released (e.g., unclosed database connections, accumulated caches, event listener leaks, or unreleased session data).","reproduction_steps":["Install or upgrade to TaskFlow v2.3 on a server with PostgreSQL on a separate host and Nginx as a reverse proxy","Start the TaskFlow server and note baseline memory usage (~500MB)","Allow normal usage by multiple users throughout the day","Monitor memory usage over several hours — expect consistent climb toward 4GB+","Observe progressive degradation in page load times and API response times as memory grows"],"environment":"TaskFlow v2.3, Ubuntu 22.04, 8GB RAM VM, PostgreSQL (separate host), Nginx reverse proxy, ~200 active users, standard install from docs","severity":"high","impact":"All ~200 active users experience degraded performance daily, with the application becoming effectively unusable by late afternoon. Requires manual daily restart as a workaround, causing downtime and operational burden.","recommended_fix":"Investigate changes introduced in v2.3 for memory management regressions. Focus on: (1) database connection pooling — verify connections to PostgreSQL are being properly returned to the pool; (2) in-memory caches or session stores that may grow unboundedly; (3) event listeners or timers that accumulate over time; (4) any new middleware or background processes added in v2.3. A heap snapshot comparison between startup and after several hours of usage would pinpoint the leaking objects.","proposed_test_case":"Run a load test simulating 200 concurrent users performing typical operations over a sustained period (e.g., 4-8 hours or accelerated via rapid request cycling). Monitor memory usage and assert that it remains within a bounded range (e.g., does not exceed 2x baseline) and does not show a monotonic upward trend. Compare results between v2.2.x and v2.3 to confirm the regression.","information_gaps":["No server-side logs or error messages available — may reveal specific warnings, OOM events, or connection pool exhaustion messages","Exact prior version before the v2.3 upgrade is unknown (needed to diff changelogs)","Runtime details unclear — reporter used 'standard install from docs' but specific Node.js/runtime version not confirmed","Whether the memory leak occurs under idle conditions or only under active user load is not yet known"]}
